Learn how to write and run end-to-end te: Learn how to write and run end-to-end tests for MBC CQRS Serverless applications using Jest and LocalStack.
Unlike unit testing, which focuses on in: Unlike unit testing, which focuses on individual modules and classes, end-to-end (e2e) testing covers the interaction of classes and modules at a more aggregate level -- closer to the kind of interaction that end-users will have with the production system. As an application grows, it becomes hard to manually test the end-to-end behavior of each API endpoint. Automated end-to-end tests help us ensure that the overall behavior of the system is correct and meets project requirements.
e2e testing tests the API in a real envi: e2e testing tests the API in a real environment so you do not need to mock any services. The main steps for writing an e2e test are:
Make API calls using the Supertest libra: Make API calls using the Supertest library to simulate HTTP requests.
Check data is correct or not: Check data is correct or not
E2E tests that share database state shou: E2E tests that share database state should run sequentially to avoid race conditions. Use the `--runInBand` flag to disable parallel execution:
Without `--runInBand`, Jest runs tests i: Without `--runInBand`, Jest runs tests in parallel across multiple workers, which can cause:
Data conflicts when tests modify the sam: Data conflicts when tests modify the same records
Flaky tests due to timing issues: Flaky tests due to timing issues
Cleanup operations affecting other runni: Cleanup operations affecting other running tests
Here is a basic structure for e2e tests:: Here is a basic structure for e2e tests:
Wait for async processing to complete: Wait for async processing to complete
Clean up test data if needed: Clean up test data if needed
The framework provides helper utilities: The framework provides helper utilities for e2e testing:
Wait for async command processing to fin: Wait for async command processing to finish

Health check from inside Docker containe: Health check from inside Docker container
Health check from GitHub Actions workflo: Health check from GitHub Actions workflow
Inside Docker containers, use service na: Inside Docker containers, use service names (e.g., dynamodb-local) to access services
In GitHub Actions workflow steps, use lo: In GitHub Actions workflow steps, use localhost (via port forwarding)
For more robust health checks, use actua: For more robust health checks, use actual API calls instead of simple connection checks (nc command)
